coredata.dc:title: Salt-resistant carbon dots modified solar steam system enhanced by chemical advection

coredata.dc:description: Salt deposition still remains a significant challenge due to the decreasing evaporation efficiency of solar desalination. Herein, we provide a salt-rejection model coupled with the experimental verification about the chemical diffusion or advection process for solar steam generation. The designed evaporating system established by in-situ polymerization (polypyrrole) of carbon dots modified MnO2 nanowires coated on non-woven cotton fabric decorated ultra-flexible and super hydrophilic polyurethane foam (30% wicking). The salt-rejection investigations reveal real-time chemical advection phenomena where hot brine flows from the top surface to underneath water through a salinity gradient with the aid of accumulated heat. The prototype demonstrates an excellent salt rejection ability within a minimum record time (2 g/100 min) or once soaked (2 g/60 s). The floatable solar-driven evaporator exhibits incredible flexibility (80%, compressive strain) along with an evaporation rate of 1.68 kg m−2 h−1, and photothermal conversion efficiency (∼96.4%, solar to vapor) from high-salinity brine (10 wt %) and wastewater containing heavy metal ions (30 wt%). The outdoor test revealed a yield of 13.60 kg m−2 in one sunny day that provides a potential solution to single-step drinking water supply from various resources of the sea, lakes, and mixtures of emulsified oil, bacterial pathogens, and industrial wastewater.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of MnCDs@PPy based solar-driven steam generation for clean water production excluding salt ions, toxic heavy metal ions, and bacterial pathogens from stimulated seawater. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The morphologies and structural investigations of CDs modified MnO2 NWs and MnCDs@PPy. FESEM images of (a) open porous structure of simple polyurethane foam, (b) super hydrophilic CDs modified MnO2 NWs, (c) PPy sprayed CDs modified MnO2 NWs, (d) MnCDs@PPy coated on polyurethane foam, (inset) showing the honeycomb-like open porous structure of MnCDs@PPy for water pathways, (e) XRD spectra of MnO2 NWs and MnCDs@PPy, (f) XPS comparative survey of MnO2 NWs and MnCDs@PPy, (g) C1S spectra and the inset shows N1S spectra of nitrogen that confirms the existence of CDs on the surface of MnO2 NWs, (h) O1S spectra of MnO2 NWs, (i) Mn2p curve of pure MnO2 NWs, and MnCDs@PPy. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The defect chemistry and a wide range of solar absorption of MnCDs@PPy based solar steam generator along with superior flexibility. (a) UV absorption spectra of MnO2 NWs, MnCDs, and MnCDs@PPy in the range of 200–2500 nm along with reflection and transmission spectrum. (b) PL spectra of MnCDs@PPy. (c) Complete band theory diagram of MnCDs@PPy, showing that CDs as a donor and MnO2 as an acceptor to enhance the photothermal conversion for steam generation. (d) Compressive stress-strain curve of MnCDs@PPy. (e) Water-droplet test along with contact angle profile. (f) Physical-mechanical test of MnCDs@PPy via twisting and pressing. (g) Comparative analysis of compressive strain vs solar absorption of MnCDs@PPy with other recent steam generators. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The scheme of MnCDs@PPy based solar steam generator. (a) The time-dependent mass changes of water, PU foam, MnCDs, and MnCDs@PPy under one sun illumination. (b) The surface temperature changes of the comparative four systems under one sun. (c) The evaporation rate and photothermal conversion efficiency of four systems under one sun. (d) The successive evaporation rate over 25 cycles showing the sustainability in steam generation. (e) The comparison of compressive strain vs photothermal efficiency of MnCDs@PPy with other recent reports. (f) The thermal conductivity of MnCDs@PPy (K < 1) reveals its insulating behavior. (g) The successive changes of the surface temperatures with increasing the irradiation times via IR camera under one sun. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The salt-resistant experiment of MnCDs@PPy based solar-driven evaporation system. (a) Time-dependent thermal dissolution of solid NaCl (2 g/100 min) under one sun illumination from the surface to the micro-porous symmetry of the MnCDs@PPy. (b) Bubble formation at the water-MnCDs@PPy interface promotes heat accumulation that is utilized for the thermal dissolution of salt ions, and the visualization of salt permeation as precipitates at the bottom surface. (c) Quick salt-rejection test via soaking without any surface deterioration. (d) No salt-accumulation on the MnCDs@PPy surface during consecutive 10 h solar-driven evaporation test under one sun illumination. (e) Concentrations of four primary salt ions before and after desalination under one sun illumination. (f) The washing degradability test reveals a smooth evaporation rate along with a salt rejection rate under 10 washing cycles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The real-time outdoor experiment of the heavy metal-resistant framework for Shahu lake water from 10:00 a.m. to 2:00 p.m. at the roof of Hubei University on September 3, 2020 (sunny day). (a–b) Schematic & roof-top illustration of MnCDs@PPy equipped with the glass condenser prototype (12 cm × 9 cm). (c) Solar irradiance, surface temperature, and evaporation rate under natural conditions during roof-top experiments. (d) Heavy metal rejection concentration before and after purification of Shahu lake water. (e) Photodegradability investigation of Shahu lake water and collected water. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a–d) Self-rejecting ability of MnCDs@PPy framework for bacterial pathogens and oil emulsions. The bacterial colony counts of agar incubated plates before and after purification under one sun (a) Shahu lake water (b) freshwater. The optical microscopies of the treated, and without treated (c) oil emulsified water (d) freshwater. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Salt-resistant carbon dots modified solar steam system enhanced by chemical advection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Salt deposition still remains a significant challenge due to the decreasing evaporation efficiency of solar desalination. Herein, we provide a salt-rejection model coupled with the experimental verification about the chemical diffusion or advection process for solar steam generation. The designed evaporating system established by in-situ polymerization (polypyrrole) of carbon dots modified MnO2 nanowires coated on non-woven cotton fabric decorated ultra-flexible and super hydrophilic polyurethane foam (30% wicking). The salt-rejection investigations reveal real-time chemical advection phenomena where hot brine flows from the top surface to underneath water through a salinity gradient with the aid of accumulated heat. The prototype demonstrates an excellent salt rejection ability within a minimum record time (2 g/100 min) or once soaked (2 g/60 s). The floatable solar-driven evaporator exhibits incredible flexibility (80%, compressive strain) along with an evaporation rate of 1.68 kg m−2 h−1, and photothermal conversion efficiency (∼96.4%, solar to vapor) from high-salinity brine (10 wt %) and wastewater containing heavy metal ions (30 wt%). The outdoor test revealed a yield of 13.60 kg m−2 in one sunny day that provides a potential solution to single-step drinking water supply from various resources of the sea, lakes, and mixtures of emulsified oil, bacterial pathogens, and industrial wastewater.

An advection-based salt-rejection model was developed for a self-floating solar desalinating device on personalized fresh water solution. The system of polypyrrole sprayed carbon dots modified MnO2 nanowires exhibits an excellent evaporation rate of 1.68 kg m−2 h−1 (∼96.4% solar to vapor efficiency) from high-salinity brine (10 wt %) and can generate 13.6 kg m2 drinking freshwater (one day) excluding heavy metal ions (30 wt %), oil emulsions, and bacterial pathogens.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Water being the rudimentary content can endanger the life of all species on its inexistence. Dwindling water reservoirs anticipate a ticking time bomb that paves the way for water scarcity on the planet. This problematic scenario demands scrutiny and encourages the scientific community to come up with valuable solutions [1,2]. In this respect, solar-driven water evaporation has validated itself as a tremendous source of clean water production owing to its maximum solar energy harvesting without any sort of mechanical input. Recently, many successful attempts were accomplished to design a highly efficient self-powered solar-driven evaporation system for clean water production to resolve the primitive water-shortage issue. These developments prevail in the heat management systems, water transport pathways, flexibility, durability, maximum solar harvesting, water-energy nexus, and origin of photothermal conversion mechanisms in photothermal materials [3–8]. Despite the amazing achievements in solar steam generation devices, its practical applications are still at the infant stage due to a significant barrier between the status quo and large-scale applications [4,9]. The latest experimental findings revealed that several photothermal designs ached from the salt accumulation and some heavy metal nanoparticle within the matrix of water transport channels that blocks steam escape channels during solar-driven steam generation [7–16]. However, multiple solutions have been developed to cope with this significant challenge but there is still a lack of comprehension of steam blocking that may lead towards low photothermal conversion efficiency and deteriorate the endurance of photothermal layers as well [17–25]. Theoretical understanding coupled with experimental investigations of salt rejection could be beneficial to develop the long-term solution of this technology [24–37]. George et al. also introduced some assumptions of chemical diffusion or advection-based simulations via selecting some wicking area to understand the real-time salt rejection mechanism using the salinity gradient [17]. Moreover, the conventional approaches to eliminate the salt accumulations via soaking and sonicating are set off the surface degradation in the form of cracks, weakens the adhesion between the photothermal layer and substrate [24–30]. Noticeably, mostly solar-driven evaporation designs are mechanically inflexible and fragile that reflects the short-term efficacy and difficulty in cleaning during the operational time. The above problem mostly emerged on hydrophobic surfaces [6,28]. To cope with this issue efficiently, the designed structure should be an ultra-flexible, thin, super hydrophilic assembly for continuous steam generation. Since super hydrophilic nature inhibits salt crystallization underneath the absorber and facilitates the movement of salt beneath the water [31–33]. Based on these merits, a stable and long-term self-desalinating solar-driven evaporation system could be established. Ultimately, the trade-off between efficiency and salt formation is the pressing challenge at present [31,32].

To best our acquaintance, crystallization takes place on the photothermal surface due to the corresponding saturation of the solution. Therefore, continuously diluted solution supplements can rinse the concentrated solution that may prevent salt accumulation [12,17,31]. Besides, the confined water supply developed to push up the efficiency but resulted in salt formation due to weak water convection [11]. Herein, we propose an efficient super hydrophilic polypyrrole sprayed carbon dots modified MnO2 nanowires (MnCDs@PPy) photothermal layer coated on ultra-flexible non-woven cotton fabric decorated with polyurethane (NPU) foam substrate. As compared to other reported evaporation systems [27,28,34–38], NPU attained super hydrophilicity and excellent permeation ability owing to its open porous structure that served as a self-powered water pumping via capillary action and wrapped NPU allowed to restrain heat within the cavity of the photothermal layer. The defect chemistry of MnCDs@PPy based steam generator plays a decisive role in the origin of the light to heat conversion process [39]. Carbon dots (CDs) exhibits a high surface to volume ratio owing to their small size and can absorb a wide range of solar spectrum due to their tightly packed energy level of loosely π electrons, to excite electrons and relax to their ground states in the form of heat generation [4,40]. The inclusion of C-dots as an effective photothermal mediator also bestowed excellent thermal stability and antifouling behavior towards salt rejection. Hu et al. also reported self-assembled carbon dots induced in the wood matrix for efficient steam generation [41]. Manganese dioxide nanowires (MnO2 NWs) show excellent absorption (UV–Vis), super hydrophilicity, refractive index (<2), and outstanding stability in water [42]. The combination of CDs and MnO2 NWs attains more considerable attention due to its unique defect chemistry, where CDs act as an electron donor and MnO2 NWs as an electron acceptor, and PPy enhances the diffused reflection within the matrix [4,39]. Zheng et al. also reported super hydrophilic MnO2 NWs and reduced graphene-based aerogels for solar-driven water desalination [42]. However, it is revealed that aerogels did not meet the mechanical strength for long-term efficacy on a commercial scale due to their weak and fragile nature and complicated fabrication procedure [43]. The modification of carbon dots enhances the superior light absorption, and the wettability of MnCDs@PPy makes it an ideal solar-powered steam generator.

In this contribution, a self-desalinating, ultra-flexible, oil-resistant, and antibacterial solar-driven evaporation system (MnCDs@PPy) is devised for clean water generation, ruling out ions, organic and inorganic pollutants. The presented work focused on the exploitation of unique super hydrophilicity of MnCDs@PPy along with continuous water pumping of NPU substrate that sieved oil and the self-rejecting capability of salts through chemical diffusion or advection mechanism. The detailed theoretical assumptions predict the migration of hot brine solution due to the salinity gradient between concentrated brine and underneath dilute solution via advection phenomena. The experimental investigation of the selected wick area (30%) followed the advective assumptions of salt rejections under continuous solar irradiance for a long-term duration. The significant heat accumulation of the wrapped NPU substrate dissolves solid NaCl into hot brine solution during continuous steam generation within the matrix of ideally porous wick structure instead of being crystallized and prevent the blockage of the vapor channel as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The MnCDs@PPy based solar-driven evaporation system is equipped with excellent light absorption (95%), self-floating ability (lightweight) along superior compressive stress-strain curve under multiple cycles (80%) that ensures its portability, durability, free-style washing ability, and endurance. The solar-driven lab experiments of MnCDs@PPy revealed an efficient evaporation rate (1.68 kg m−2 h−1) and excellent solar to vapor conversion efficiency (96.4%) under one sun. The system exhibits the lowest thermal conductivity (0.0576 W m−1 K−1), which is far less than that of standard water thermal conductivity (0.6 W m−1 K−1). The detailed salt-rejection experiments (2 g/100 min or soaked once for 1 min) and oil emulsified water investigations depicts the self-rejecting capability MnCDs@PPy that promotes stable evaporation fortified with zero contact angle. Mainly, the real-time outdoor experiment of MnCDs@PPy along with glass solar condenser (12 × 9 cm) was successfully employed to yield freshwater (13.6 kg m−2) from Shahu lake muddy water in one sunny day, showing the ability to filter out heavy toxic heavy metal’s ions, and bacterial pathogens from lake wastewater.

All the chemicals MnSO4·H2O, CH3COONa, and KClO3 have acquired Wuhan BASF Chemical Industries Co, Ltd., whereas while ethanol and acidic corrosive were gained from Wuhan Guangfu fine synthetic industry. Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) and phytic acid (PA, 50 wt% in water) were offered by J & K. Pyrrole, ammonium persulfate, and isopropanol alcohol (IPA) were bought from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. O-phenylenediamine, lignin, terpineol (C10H18O), and absolute ethanol was sold by Aladdin industrial corporation (shanghai china). All the received chemicals were sustaining a purity level of 99% and were processed without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MnO2 NWs and surface casing
ce:section.ce:para: Fixed amounts of 0.6870 g CH3COOK, 0.8579 g KClO3, and 0.6761 g MnSO4·H2O were taken and stirred into 60 mL distilled water until it was completely dissolved. After preparing a fine mixture, it was mixed with 3.2 mL CH3COOH and stirred continuously until it reached to homogenous state. Afterward, this solution was put in the oven after pouring into the 100 mL stainless steel autoclave at a temperature of 160 °C for 12 h for the sake of completion of the reaction. After that, the obtained paste is taken out and allowed to be cooled freely at room temperature. The following element was gained by suction filtration. To neutralize and purify the resulting paste, it was washed with absolute ethanol and distilled water several times. After washing, it was dried at 60 °C, MnO2 NWs were collected. The obtained MnO2 NWs were then immersed for 5 min in the PA solution (40 mg mL−1, prepared by addition of 2.95 mL of the 50 wt% PA solution to 44.66 mL of distilled water). Subsequently, PA solutions were mixed with 8.40 mL of FeCl3·6H2O solution (10 mg mL−1) to produce the following final concentrations: FeCl3·6H2O, 1.5 mg mL−1, and PA, 1.5 mg mL−1. After 1 min, the super hydrophilic customized nanowire was taken out, washed properly with water, and used nitrogen gas to dry it thoroughly.

Functionalized CDs were synthesized using a one-pot solvothermal technique. Carbon sourced, O-phenylenediamine (30 mg), and lignin (10 mg) were dissolved in absolute ethanol (10 mL) and stirred vigorously for 60 min using a magnetic stirrer. The obtained light brownish homogeneous solution was transferred into a Teflon vessel within a tightly sealed stainless-steel autoclave at 180 °C for 10 h. The autoclave allowed to cool naturally and obtained dark yellow solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove the massive particle (endapan) from the final product, and finally, the supernatant was collected.

The 1.2 g small-sized MnO2 NWs and 2 mL solution of functionalized CDs were mixed using a magnetic stirrer for 30 min and transferred to the hydrothermal route for 3 h at 150 °C and allowed to cool naturally. The obtained product was washed several times with DI water until to achieve pH 7. The collected, modified MnO2 NWs were mixed into well-known binder terpineol (C10H18O) using a mortar pestle to make a slurry for coating. The super hydrophilic and self-floating polyurethane foam is covered with non-woven cotton fabric selected as a substrate and designed into a circular shape (2 cm × 2 cm × 2 cm = 8 cm3). The obtained dark grey gel was coated uniformly on circular shaped polyurethane foam using a BOSOBO paintbrush. Afterward, the MnCDs coated polyurethane foam-based device was kept in a microwave oven at 120 °C for 50 min to remove the binder. The same procedure was repeated for simple MnO2 NWs coated on polyurethane foam to analyze the comparison with functional carbon dots. The collected products were presented for investigation of solar-driven based steam generation.

In order to enhance the diffuse reflection (minimizing the refractive index) and solar absorption, polypyrrole coatings were prepared using a single step in situ polymerization [42]. In polymerization, 0.85 mL pyrrole was dissolved in 5 mL isopropanol alcohol (IPA) and trailed through the addition of 1.85 mL phytic acid (50%, wt% in water) using a magnetic stirrer until to achieve dark brownish color which shows homogeneous solution (solution A). The 2.75 g ammonium persulfate was mixed vigorously into 5 mL distilled water until to get a transparent solution (solution B). Both solutions (A & B) were collected into a handy spray can. The fabricated MnCDs photothermal layer was sprayed by the following sequence of first solution A and then solution B. The successive repetition was followed until the homogeneous and dark black surface of MnCDs@ PPy was achieved. The comparative investigations of the surface of the MnCDs samples were carried out before spraying and after the loading of polypyrrole. The concentration of PPy has an estimated 20 mg on a circular shaped (8 cm3) MnCDs@PPy solar-driven prototype. The schematic illustration of the complete synthesis and fabrication process of MnCDs@PPy is illustrated in Fig. S1. The material characterizations are also explained in the Supporting Information Note (Note S1).

All the glassware and media were autoclaved at 120 °C for 30 min, sterilized the material by disinfectant, and dried in the sterilized area before any experiment. The collected water samples directly pour (1 mL) on the prepared sterile nutrient agar plates and spread evenly. Incubated the plate at 37 °C for 48 h and count the number of colonies on the plate. On the other hand, purified water with the help of sterilized material has also proceeded in the same way. For the bacterial count, the colony-forming unit is an efficient method through this method; the bioburden can be determined. However, an agar well-diffusion based antibacterial test was also performed using both gram-positive (Escherichia coli, Bacillus subtilis) and gram-negative (Staphylococcus aureus, Staphylococcus aureus) bacterial strains. For this, 20 μg mL−1 of MnO2, CDs, and MnCDs@PPy powder in DMSO and sonicate it for 1 h to homogenize in DMSO and store the mixture with proper labels in sterilized tubes.

The experiments on solar steam generation were performed through a solar simulator (PLS-FX300HU) enabled to provide the simulated solar intensity of 1 kW m−2 or one sun. The optical filter equipped with a standard 1.5 G AM spectrum was utilized. The MnCDs@PPy based solar-driven device covering 30 mm thickness was enabled to float on the pure water or (simulated seawater, shah lake water, and other organic or oil emulsified solutions) poured in a beaker that was exposed under a 1 kW m−2 solar flux beam spot. The electronic analytical balance (Mettler Toledo, ME204) fortified with 0.001 g resolution was used to analyze the time-dependent mass change. Subsequently, all evaporation rates were recorded for 60 min under one sun illumination after stabilization. The infrared thermal imaging camera (FLIR E4 Pro, America) armed with two thermocouples were positioned on the top and bottom surface of MnCDs@PPy. The comparative analysis of salt and heavy metal ions concentrations in source and condensed water was conducted using inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, E.P. Optimal 8000). Collectively, all the tests were made at an ambient temperature of ∼22 °C in the presence of ∼44% of humidity. The Hand-Held Optical Meter Model, along with thermocouples, was utilized to record the surface temperature.

The microstructures and morphologies of as-prepared MnO2 NWs, CDs modified MnO2 NWs, and fabricated MnCDs@PPy photothermal layers were investigated using FESEM, EDAX, TEM. Fig. 2 a illustrates the uniform open porous structure and vertically aligned micro-channel of polyurethane (PU) foam. The open porous assembly of PU foam enabled its flexible and super hydrophilic passage for continuous water transport. Fig. 2b represents the well-aligned, ultra-long crystalline structure of CDs modified MnO2 NWs that are intervened with each other, the 10 nm-sized CDs embedded uniformly on the 70 nm diameter surface of MnO2 NWs. The wide diameter of hydrothermally grown and surface cladded MnO2 NWs boosts the hydrophilicity, wettability, porosity, and enhances the concentration of embedded CDs on the surface of MnO2 NWs. Fig. S2 & S3 demonstrates surface morphologies of the surface cladded pure MnO2 NWs and CDs modified MnO2 NWs via FESEM and TEM as provided in Supporting Information (SI). As reported in our group [44], functionalized CDs with a size of approximately 10 nm was utilized for the modification of MnO2 NWs to achieve the maximum solar absorption and attains more excellent wettability, as shown in Fig. S4 (SI). Furthermore, element dispersion spectrum (EDS) analysis was also carried out to investigate the elemental composition of as-synthesized MnO2 NWs and after surface modification of nanowires by CDs. The mapping of the MnCDs@PPy solar-driven evaporation system reveals the existence of Mn, O, C, and N, respectively which shows the intervening of nanowires with each other, and existing of CDs on MnO2 NWs surface, as shown in Fig.S5 (SI). Moreover, the inclusion of polypyrrole (PPy) on the surface of CDs modified MnO2 NWs could be seen in Fig. 3 c. The 80 nm sized PPy nanoparticles equally distribute along with 70 nm diameter MnO2 NWs that confirmed the desired composition of MnCDs@PPy. However, hydrophilic PPy spraying on super hydrophilic MnO2 NWs reduces the surface tension of the nanowires, and it gives an advantage for water droplets to transport easily. Fig. 2d represents the surface morphology of MnCDs@PPy deposited on polyurethane foam, and it can be seen clearly, how CDs modified MnO2 NWs immensely distributed on the polyurethane surface. Fig. 2d (inset), revealing the honeycomb-like microstructure of MnCDs@PPy where PPy spraying homogeneously covered the surface of CDs modified MnO2 NWs coated on polyurethane foam and its surface roughness was also enhanced to appeal to diffuse reflection and wide range of solar absorption [39,42].

Fig. 2e demonstrates the XRD patterns of pure MnO2 NWs and MnCDs@PPy in which all the reflections are perfectly matched with standard data (JCPDS 00-024-0735). The crystal structural analysis reveals the crystalline tetragonal structure of pure MnO2 NWs, and two other peaks appear in the MnCDs@PPy spectrum corresponding to CDs. All diffraction peaks positioned at the angles of 19.8°, 22.8°, 28.6°, 37.5°, 41.0°, 42.8°, 56.7°, and 59.4° with the corresponding index planes (002), (110), (101), (200), (111), (211), and (220), respectively. The broad diffraction peak appeared at 22.7° for MnCDs@PPy reveals that mostly the concentration of CDs is attached to the surface of ultralong MnO2 NWs [39]. The same crystallographic results are reported in Haipeng et al. [39]. The results exposed 10% of the element carbon in MnO2 NWs, which is consistent with the results of the EDS that is depicted the elemental composition as Mn (45%), O (44%), C (10%), and N (1%) respectively as shown in Fig. S6 (SI). In order to explore the effect of modification of functionalized CDs on MnO2 NWs, an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigation was also carried out to identify the chemical valence state, composition of MnO2 NWs. It is clear evidence of the existence of nitrogen peaks that is the identification of CDs in XPS spectra of MnCDs@PPy as compared to pure MnO2 NWs, as illustrated in Fig. 2f. The XPS survey spectra exhibit that the following peaks of Mn2p (645 eV), O1s (532 eV), N1s (399 eV), and C1s (285 eV) can be seen in MnCDs@PPy photothermal layer. The C1s spectrum of MnCDs@PPy shows three prominent peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.7 eV, and 288.4 eV, which attributes to the bonding of C–C/CC, C–O, and OC–O groups, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 2g. Besides, the inset in Fig. 2g demonstrates that the presence of N1s reveals the presence of functionalized CDs on the surface of MnO2 NWs, and the CDs enhance the wettability and act as an electron donor for an acceptor MnO2 NWs. Fig. 2h shows the O1s spectrum of the MnCDs@PPy photothermal layer, showing three peaks that correspond to the following oxygen bonded groups Mn–O–Mn (BE = 530 eV), Mn–OH/CO (BE = 531.8 eV), and C–OH/C–O–C (BE = 532.6 eV), respectively. In contrast, the first two peaks in the O1s spectrum correspond to pure MnO2 NWs, and the third peak C–O–C approves the existence of CDs, which disperse on the surface of MnO2 NWs and enhance the wettability of MnO2 NWs [39]. Moreover, the Mn2p curve of pure ultralong MnO2 NWs and MnCDs@PPy reveals the spin-orbit peaks at 642 eV to Mn2p3∖2 while 654.2 eV to Mn2p1∖2, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 2i.

The intrinsic defects of the host material produce maximum phonon vibrations by capturing electrons in their defect states and loses their energy in the form of heat. Surface defects play an important role in light scattering on the surface of photothermal layers. The light scattering lead towards irregular diffuse reflection, which intervened inside the atmosphere of material and localized itself within the matrix [31–35]. To quantify the superior light absorption of the MnCDs@PPy photothermal layer in the wide range of the solar spectrum (200–2500 nm), UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to analyze the maximum solar absorption and a better understanding of the solar-driven evaporation system. Fig. 3a demonstrates the UV–Vis absorption spectra of simple MnO2 NWs, MnCDs, and MnCDs@PPy, revealing the outstanding absorption (95%) along with minimum reflection (3%) and transmission (2%) followed by the exact absorption formula (1 - T - R). As functionalized CDs are embedded on the surface of MnO2 NWs, the CDs act like an electron donor while MnO2 NWs an electron acceptor [39]. Fig. 3b represents the PL spectrum of MnCDs@PPy, exhibiting strong sharp peaks at 441, 463, 479, 537, 557, 584, 608, and 638 nm, that corresponds to the respective bandgaps 2.81, 2.65, 2.60, 2,30, 2.22, 2.12, 2.03, and 1.94 eV, respectively. The excessive strong, sharp peaks emerged due to the origin of the quantum effects of functionalized CDs modified MnO2 NWs [39]. The UV absorption peak at 441 eV corresponds to the radiative recombination of MnO2 NWs while the blue region emission peak at 463 nm and yellow region peaks at 557 nm attribute to the surface defects that are caused by the dangling bonds of CDs, where electron excited from LUMO to HUMO band [39].

As illustrated in Fig. 3c, the excited electron migrates from a higher HUMO level to a lower level, and it radiates excessive absorbed energy in the form of heat and then transfers to the conduction band of MnO2 NWs. The strong green region absorption peaks at 479 and 537 nm corresponds to the point defect such as oxygen vacancies, which play a vital role in the acceptor response of MnO2 NWs. These oxygen vacancies act like a hoping charge carrier and capture electrons. When an electron migrates from the CDs HUMO band to the conduction band of MnO2 NWs, and it releases heat in the form of phonon vibrations [39,42]. The other three peaks at 584, 608, and 638 nm reveal the intrinsic interstitial defects present in the MnCDs@PPy photothermal layer, and they give birth to phonon lattice vibrations. They may lead to heat generation via defect chemistry. These interstitial defects act as trap states between the conduction and valence bands where the excited electron trap in these interstitial sites and generate phonon vibrations in the form of heat. Thus, more phonon vibration enhances the photothermal conversion behavior of a solar-driven evaporation system [39,42].

Super hydrophilic water pathways play an essential role in allowing smooth water transportation. Extraordinary efforts have been employed in our group to develop a mechanically robust and hydrophilic system that owes to excellent wettability and natural salt-rejection ability [17,36–40]. Fig. 3d represents the compressive stress-strain curve of the MnCDs@PPy system, revealing the superior mechanical strength of 0.8 MPa under 80% strain. This huge compressibility factor enhances the physical endurance to avoid cracks on the surface and long-term flexibility of the device. No cracks and surface degradation have been seen under 0.8 Mpa modulus intensity. The real-time visualization of the surface wettability of MnCDs@PPy through direct contact with the water drop test is shown in Fig. 3e and Video S1(SI). The quick absorption of water confirmed the existence of a microchannel water path, which is followed by the capillary action force. The successive dynamic contact angle test was also performed, as demonstrated in Supporting Information (Video S2). It reveals that a well-oriented microchannel is beneficial for water transport, and its aligned skeleton is capable of dissolving salt ions within the matrix to avoid any blockage of capillary action [44,45]. Moreover, the surface contact angle investigation shows the zero-contact angle between the water droplet and the surface of MnCDs@PPy, as illustrated in Fig. 3e (inset), confirming the super hydrophilicity and wettability of the surface of the fabricated solar-driven system. It was found that water is quickly immersed into the bottom layer of polyurethane when water droplets hit the surface of the MnCDs@PPy layer, showing that there is no need for the wicking effect to create the wettability between the interfaces of non-woven decorated PU substrate and MnCDs@PPy. The real-time flexibility and mechanical robustness of MnCDs@PPy based solar-driven evaporation system via twisting and pressing without any material degradation could be visualized from Fig. 3f and video S3 (SI). The superior compressive strain (80% recovery) of MnCDs@PPy promotes outstanding flexibility that enhances washing endurance cycles without any color or MnCDs@PPy adhesion deterioration as compared to other nanofibers, aerogels, and other polymer foam-based steam generators [8,9,27,38,40]. However, the comparison of compressive strain vs solar absorption ability of MnCDs@PPy with other solar-driven steam generators is illustrated in Fig. 3g. The air permeability was also examined using the airflow rate passing perpendicularly across both MnCDs@PPy coated polyurethane foam and without deposition. Fig. S7 (SI) demonstrates the air permeabilities of pristine polyurethane foam, and MnCDs@PPy coated polyurethane foam, estimated as 86.3 ± 1.6 mm s−1, and 77.1 ± 2.3 mm s−1, respectively.

Versatile MnCDs@PPy along with solar absorption (95%) enables it to perform as an efficient solar-driven evaporation system. Herein, we compared four systems for the photothermal conversion process, which includes pure water, polyurethane foam, MnCDs, and MnCDs@PPy. The time-dependent mass change of these designated four systems was recorded by a weighing balance under one sun illumination (1 kW m−2). After 1 h of solar irradiation, the MnCDs@PPy shows the maximum mass change (2.12 kg m−2), as illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The mass change using MnCDs@PPy was also recorded under different solar illumination up to 3 kW m−2, as represented in Fig. S8 (SI). The detailed comparison of mass change with other successful attempts was provided in Table S1 (SI). As shown in Fig. 4b, the surface temperature of four developed evaporation systems including pure water (21.2 °C), PU foam (34.4 °C), MnCDs (40.8 °C), and MnCDs@PPy (48.5 °C) under one sun illumination. However, the surface temperature of MnCDs@PPy inclined with concerning the solar intensity and achieved 67.6 °C under 3 sun in 1 h, as shown in Fig. S9 (SI). The c/omparative evaporation rates of these four systems are shown in Fig. 4c, revealing that the evaporation rate of MnCDs@PPy (1.68 kg m−2 h−1) and photothermal conversion efficiency (96.4%), which is higher than that of the reported MnO2 NWs [41], and many other solar-driven systems [10,15,46–48]. The photothermal conversion efficiency (ŋ) of the MnCDs@PPy solar-driven evaporation system was measured by the followed equation [49].

However, C is the specific heat capacity of water (4.2 kJ kg−1 K−1) while △T represents the gradual increase of water temperature. The humidity was recorded 44% approximately, and the environment temperature was 22.3 ± 1 °C during solar-driven experiments. Followed these above equations, MnCDs@PPy can generate vapor at 45.8 °C with the corresponding photothermal conversion efficiency (96.4%) excluding the evaporation rate measured in the dark to insulate the precise effect of solar input, and heat conduction losses, e.g., QConduction (5.64%), QConvection (1.67%), and QRadiation (1.47%) heat losses to the environment. The detailed heat losses and photothermal conversion efficiency calculations are provided in Supporting Information Note. S2.

The MnCDs@PPy is efficient for solar-driven evaporation, and the evaporation rate after 25 cycles shows negligible variation, and it holds the evaporation rate between 1.66 and 1.68 kg m−2 h−1, as shown in Fig. 4d. Furthermore, the mass change profile also remains almost the same (2.12 kg m−2) in 25 repetitive evaporation cycles, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 4d. The superior compressive strain (80% recovery) and open porous structure of MnCDs@PPy promote the continuous steam generation and achieved outstanding photothermal conversion efficiency as compared to other nanofibers, aerogels, and other polymer foam-based steam generators [8,9,27,38,40]. Thermal management plays a crucial role in increasing the overall efficiency of the solar-driven evaporation system [30,32,49]. Non-woven fabric wrapped across the walls of polyurethane foam (Fig. S10) shows tremendous thermal insulation, and it plays a vital role in building efficient heat accumulation for an efficient solar-driven evaporation system [50]. Herein, the thermal properties of the fabricated solar-driven evaporation system were practically conducted using a well-known thermal conductivity meter (Hot Disk, TPS 2500, Sweden hot disk cooperation). When the power supply is switched on then a series of the temperature gradient (dT/dx) would be established in a vertical direction. Fourier equations comprehend the permeation of heat transfer via the MnCDs@PPy system, as mentioned below [49].

Here k1 is the thermal conductivity (1.05 W m-1 K−1), d1 is the thickness of the glass slide (3 mm), while d2 is the MnCDs@PPy thickness (30 mm). As shown in Fig. 4e, MnCDs@PPy exhibits the lowest thermally conductivity (0.0576 ± 0.002 W m−1 K−1), which is 10 times less than that of water (0.6 W m−1 K−1). To examine the thermal insulation capabilities of MnCDs@PPy along the microchannel direction, the thermal images were captured using an infrared camera (FLIR E8, USA). The instant temperature change of the heat-insulating matrix was also recorded via I.R. camera under one sun illumination, as demonstrated in Fig. 4f. The temperature increases over time and attains approximately 48.5 °C within 20 min. The obtained lowest thermal conductivity enables MnCDs@PPy to restrain the temperature on the surface and does not allow to conduct to bulk water. It is beneficial for an efficient solar-driven steam generation as anticipated in Video S4 & S5 (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Salt rejection via chemical diffusion or advection
ce:section.ce:para: The designed evaporation system exhibits partial hydrophobic wrapping to the open pore bottom polyurethane layer. It restricts heat at the top interfacial layer and limits to conduct to bulk water, and it is beneficial for an efficient solar-driven evaporation system [18,49,50]. To demonstrate the salt rejection ability of MnCDs@PPy in minimum record time, the following experiment was conducted. For this, 2.0 g solid NaCl (26 wt%) was placed at the top surface of MnCDs@PPy, and the evaporation system floated on 3.5 wt% of NaCl simulated seawater. The 26 wt% of solid NaCl is enough to saturate the whole wick structure. Subsequently, the system was illuminated under 1 kW m−2 solar irradiance, revealing that the solid NaCl disappears from the surface via continuous steam flow through a well-aligned open porous structure of MnCDs@PPy after consecutive 100 min. As anticipated in Fig. 5 a, the MnCDs@PPy system shows a quick salt rejection ability under 1 kW m−2 solar illumination. The wrapping of non-woven fabric strongly affects the wettability of the bottom layer and generates some bubbles at a liquid-air interface, where migration of concentrated hot brine solution could be seen clearly through the salinity gradient followed by advection assumptions, as illustrated in Fig. 5b, and Video S6 (SI). The generation of these bubbles at the bottom layer of MnCDs@PPy confirms the heat sustaining the ability of MnCDs@PPy that boosts the steam generation of the device and avoid any salt accumulation. The superior flexibility of MnCDs@PPy provides free-style washing ability and salt-accumulated surface could be rinsed via soaking for 1 min without appearing any surface or color deterioration, as illustrated in Fig. 5c. Super hydrophilic, ultra-flexible, and efficient MnCDs@PPy based system shows no salt-accumulation during consecutive 10 h solar-driven evaporation test reveals its long-term functionality, as demonstrated in Fig. 5d. Both experiments revealed that (1) the ultra-flexible structure of MnCDs@PPy can dissolve and rejects salt within the matrix under continuous sunlight illumination, and (2) solid NaCl could be dissolved or rejected for quite a few days during solar-driven evaporation.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.01.140

Basically, salt accumulation occurs during evaporation time but it diffuses back into underneath water via the porous assembly of the evaporation system. Indeed, the rate of accumulation should not be greater than the rate of diffusion [11,12,17]. However, diffusion and advection play a decisive role in salt rejection ability. In order to comprehend the salt-rejection ability of MnCDs@PPy, the theoretical approach was also examined by employing Fick’s diffusion law [12].

According to equation (vii), salt rejection purely depends on the porosity and thickness of water transport material. However, the ratio of water transport and diffusion area to evaporation rate is equal. Indeed, higher porosity, minimum water transport material thickness, and improvement in heat insulation could enhance the salt rejection efficiency of a solar-driven steam generator [17–32]. For this, we have chosen non-woven fabric for heat insulation and 30% of the wick area for diffusion assumption. It was found that the NaCl concentration was comparatively low on the top of the wick for saturated conditions, signifying the further mass transfer mechanism should be implemented [17]. We tried to reveal this salt gradient through advection. The detailed theoretical analysis of both assumptions along with schematic illustrations (Fig. S11) is provided in the Supporting Information Note (Note S3). The characteristic parameter of MnCDs@PPy determines whether salt rejection followed diffusion or advection. The salt rejection scenario of both mechanisms along with low heat losses is anticipated below.
(8)

Here Qd represents the heat losses through heat conduction with bulk water while Jd indicates the salt rejection via chemical diffusion of NaCl. The Qconv and Jconv correspond to heat losses and salt rejection of NaCl through the hot brine advection mechanism, while
∝ w
is the thermal diffusivity of water. In the diffusion process, salt diffusion followed by the concentration gradient, and heat conduct from the top photothermal surface to underneath water. In contrast, a volume of concentrated hot brine is exchanged with comparatively diluted seawater [17,32]. Hence, the inclusion of heat-insulated non-woven fabric reduces heat losses, and the choice of advection boosts the salt-rejecting ability of MnCDs@PPy for the continuous flow of steam generation.

Inductive coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) was employed to investigate the concentration gradient of four different salt ions (3.5 W% NaCl) within stimulated seawater and condensed pure freshwater. The detailed desalination comparison shows that the concentrations of Na1+, K1+, Ca2+, and Mg2+ ions significantly reduced after purification in contrast with the amount of salt ions in the simulated seawater, as illustrated in Fig. 5e. The obtained desalinated water approach more than 99% purity, rejecting salt ions, which is fully meet the drinking water quality criteria of the World Health Organization [1,48,51]. The pure condensed water exhibits the ingredients of standard water, and it can be utilized for drinking water supply. Noticeably, another flexibility test of MnCDs@PPy was carried out in terms of washing cycles vs salt rejection time, and evaporation rate. Fig. 5f demonstrates the salt rejecting durability (2 g NaCl/100 min @ 1 sun) along with a smooth evaporation rate (1.68 kg m−2 h−1) under consecutive 10 washing cycles. MnCDs@PPy shows no surface or color degradation over several washing cycles as briefly discussed in the above sections.

The MnCDs@PPy is efficient for the treatment of industrial lake wastewater treatment. The muddy industrial wastewater was taken from Shahu lake, located in Wuhan, China. The obtained muddy wastewater was examined briefly, using ICP-OES spectroscopy to analyze the concentrations of primary ions and heavy metals present in Shahu lake wastewater. The detailed investigations revealed the presence of an immense amount of salt calcium ions and numerous amounts of heavy metal ions such as Ca2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ti3+, Hg2+, Fe3+, and Zn2+ ions. The roof-top experiment was successfully employed from 10:00 a.m. to 2:00 p.m. at the roof of Hubei University on September 3, 2020 (sunny day). The heat insulation and water collection protocol for MnCDs@PPy were followed as described in a schematic illustration, as shown in Fig. 6 a. The global solar irradiance or solar intensity was measured using Hukseflux LP-02 thermal pyranometer. The sloped glass condenser (12 cm × 9 cm) was oriented towards the south, and the beaker was filled several times with Shahu lake water muddy water, as shown in Fig. 6b. Moreover, mass change, surface temperature, solar irradiance, humidity, and collected water concentrations were recorded throughout the day. Fig. 6c demonstrate the roof-top solar-driven evaporation profile of the MnCDs@PPy system in which surface temperature and the evaporation rate is purely depending on incident solar intensity. The maximum solar intensity was recorded at 955 W m-2 during post meridian while the surface temperature was recorded (48 °C) and maximum evaporation (1.68 kg m−2 h−1) achieved. The efficiency of clean water collection could be evaluated from this equation.

which is the time-dependent solar irradiance while the denominator belongs to the whole solar insulation of the day. Using this equation, MnCDs@PPy along with a sloped glass condenser (12 cm × 9 cm) can produce 13.6 kg m−2 freshwater in one sunny day excluding heavy metals ions along with the rejection of heavy concentration of Ca2+ ions, and bacterial pathogens in Shahu lake water that is quite better than conventional solar stills.

The nano-filtration capability for heavy metal ions in Shahu lake water was also investigated using ICP-OES spectroscopy. Fig. 6d and S12 (SI) illustrated the heavy metal rejection ability of MnCDs@PPy where a higher concentration of Ca2+ ions in Shahu lake water was significantly reduced in condensed water. Moreover, the Pb2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ti3+, Hg2+, Fe3+, and Zn2+ ions rejected successfully, and the collected water meets the standard of drinking quality according to the World Health Organization (WHO) standard.

The photodegradability is the utmost challenge in reverse osmosis or membrane system. The selection of Shahu lake wastewater aimed to investigate the real-time investigation of the photodegradability test. The UV-based photodegradability test of Shahu lake muddy water before and after treatment, as shown in Fig. 6e. The inset image illustrates the color comparison of Shahu lake’s muddy water and purified fresh condensed water. After desalination, Shahu lake (SW) becomes colorless, and its absorption peak is completely removed. It is approaching zero absorption, which reveals its absence of contamination of Shahu lake water, i.e., primary ions and heavy metals ions. Hence, it is evident that the MnCDs@PPy system exhibits the real-time photodegradability ability for drinkable clean water production.

According to statistics from the WHO, 80% of the diseases are transmitted by consuming contaminated water linked to bacterial micro-organisms [1,48–52]. Although most of these micro-organisms are present in low concentrations, many of which are the cause of toxicological considerations, especially wastewater, present as components of complex mixtures, and it is the main cause of some most dangerous bacterial infections around the globe [1,20,32,53,54]. In this contribution, Shahu lake water samples from China (Wuhan) were collected and processed throughout this process. 1 mL sample was carried out in a sterilized Petri plate through a pour plate method. It is shown that when the untreated water was incubated at 37 °C, there are many observable colonies showed on the surface of the agar plates. But after purification, negligible colonies were seen on the surface plate under the limit of the national drinking water Standard (GB5749-85), as shown in Fig. 7 a and b. The results of both plates show that microbial load can be removed through the purification of water and seems to produce fresh, safe drinking water through this process. Furthermore, the nanoparticle-based antibacterial mechanism is still hypothetically towards the bactericidal effect, and it is not fully understood up till now. Different mechanisms have been proposed for the antibacterial effect of nanoparticles. Except for a large area/volume ratio of green synthesized nanoparticles exhibits better contact with bacterial cells and the antibacterial effect due to oxidative stress caused by the free state radical formation [55–61]. The bioactivity of individual MnO2 NPs, CDs, and MnCDs@PPy against four bacterial pathogens were examined, as illustrated in Fig. S13 (SI). The detailed investigations regarding inhibitory zones of individual concentrations of MnO2 NWs, CDs, MnCDs, and MnCDs@PPy are provided in Table. S2 (SI).

To diversify our evaporation system, oil emulsified water solutions were also tested to analyze the separation of oil contamination from industrial wastewater, as shown in Fig. 7c and d. Recent research deeply stressed this issue to clean up oil-contaminated wastewater and declared it a crucial point. The efficient separation of emulsified oil/water is essential for the purification of oily industrial wastewater. We took the samples of surfactant-stabilized emulsion (SSE) as models to assess the separation efficiency of the MnCDs@PPy-based solar-driven system. The SSE, water-oil drop with the sizes of 10–20 μm were emulsified on water. After purification, there are no clear drops to be detected throughout the sight of the SEE. So, the collected water achieved a high purity of 99%. Compared to some business polymer filters available, MnCDs@PPy can be utilized stable under hard organic media, making it a promising material treatment of oily industrial wastewater.

The presented design and experimental investigations demonstrate the novel ultra-flexible and advective salt-resistant solar-driven evaporating system, where thermal management of MnCDs@PPy sustain heat localization and its open porous assembly provide smooth pathways for mass transfer of hot brine solution towards the dilute solution of stimulated seawater. Many salt rejections experiments have been carried out in labs and oceans as well [12–23,28,43]. The structure or designs of solar-driven systems decides whether salt-rejection followed chemical diffusion or advection phenomena. Indeed, the fluid thermodynamics model could be established to comprehend the real scenario for the development of future work [12,17]. A slope-shaped glass collector was fabricated and coupled with an evaporating system for collecting freshwater from a different source of water such as Shahu lake muddy water, oil-emulsified water, and stimulated seawater. The evaporation rate was examined critically under lab experiments and natural solar irradiance. Fascinatingly, there was no significant variation in surface temperature, and the evaporation rate has been recorded under the same solar intensity. The evaporation rate (1.68 kg m−2 h−1) was similar under the same conditions concerning lab experiments. Although polyurethane has been already reported [45,62], we have tried enough to improve its adhesive characteristics with photothermal layers using an excellent volatile binder (terpineol). Many researchers including our group contribute to this domain such as introducing a silk fabric-based photothermal layer wrapped over PU foam [62], but still, there was a gap as two-channel-based indirect water transportation [11,15]. In other words, water transportation was carried out first from PU foam then separated photothermal absorber where enough wicking effect much needed [4–13,24,31,63]. To cope with this issue and simplify the device structure, a semi-direct water contact approach is developed. We have tried enough to highlights the ultra-flexibility of polyurethane foam and its indirect contact with water using non-woven covering to minimize the heat losses. Interestingly, several washing cycles and salt-rejection experiments do not affect the adhesion between MnCDs@PPy and NPU foam. The huge compressive stress-strain profile of the designed photothermal system appeals to the maximum durability and portability of the device’s nature. In recent studies, the hydrophilicity of the polyurethane substrate may be affected due to the hydrophobic nature of deposited solar-absorbing materials [50]. The selection of super hydrophilic MnO2 NWs sustains the hydrophilicity as the incorporation of CDs enhances the wettability and the entire range of solar absorption. The defect chemistry of super hydrophilic MnO2 NWs and functionalized CDs comprehend the photothermal conversion phenomena [39]. The PL and XPS spectroscopy reveal that oxygen vacancies or point defects exist in the MnO2 NWs structure that leads towards non-relaxation behavior with the aid of excessive free electrons from CDs electron-donor. Carbon dots were used as photothermal materials due to the large surface area, ease of functionalization, improved antifouling behaviors, superb mechanical strength, excellent sieving capabilities, and exceptional water transport properties. The water molecules that kinetically leave the freshwater layer can either be part of the vapor or form islands/droplets on the carbon surface. Previous studies have shown both experimentally and theoretically to be a favored process even though the carbon surface is slightly hydrophobic. It was attributed to the fact that most of the hydrogen bonds are satisfied in the island/droplet configuration, thereby lowering its overall energy [64]. Such droplets/islands are then fast-transported on the MnCDs@PPy surface to form the freshwater flux, driven by the chemical potential gradient. The combination of MnO2 NWs and CDs appeals to additional aspects such as photodegradation of Shahu lake water and higher wettability could be utilized in various applications such as energy storage devices etc.

There are ample improvements required to meet its practical applications that are still at the infant stage due to a significant barrier between the status quo and large-scale applications. Low-cost collectors along with higher optical transparency modification may contribute towards a sustainable and easy approach to produce clean drinking water [12,17]. The droplet formation on the steam collectors equipped with minimum optical losses could be beneficial for higher efficiency [12,65]. Moreover, another area of improvement in this technology could be a less wicking area because of backside heat losses [12,17]. Although, we have chosen 30% of the wicking area of the water-transport channel for hot-brine regulation via the advection assumption that is revealed in our salt-rejecting experiments. The whole wick area was treated as a conservative convention of diffusion-based salt-resistant assembly. The width of the wicking area plays a significant role in the dominance of mass transfer or advective flow of hot brine as compared to salt-accumulation within the wicking matrix. There is an upper limit of width of the wicking area which should not be too wide or narrow [17,66]. If all the above challenges are addressed efficiently then our designed system can be predicted the maximum output of clean water production especially for 785 billion people of the world (living without clean drinking water, according to WHO) [48,67].

In summary, a versatile salt-resistant framework of MnCDs@PPy has been developed for quick clean water production equipped with the excellent rejection ability of salt accumulation, oil-emulsions, and bacterial pathogens. Salt-rejection experiments fortified with theoretical assumptions depict the suitable understanding of the advection mechanism where hot-brine solution migrated through thermal insulating NPU substrate to underneath simulated seawater. The extreme flexibility endorses the versatility of a salt-resistant framework where excellent durability, portability, and a wide range of solar harvesting is achieved. The photothermal conversion phenomena of MnCDs@PPy is purely based on the defect chemistry of functionalized CDs modified MnO2 NWs. The electron donor CDs facilitate the electron acceptor MnO2 NWs to capture electrons in their trap states. Thus, these trap states enable the excited electrons to emit phonons in the form of lattice vibrations (heat) to achieve excellent photothermal conversion efficiency (96.4%) along with an excellent evaporation rate (1.68 kg m−2 h−1). The outdoor experiment employed on Shahu lake water entitled with an efficient yield of pure drinking water (13.6 kg m−2) in one sunny day equipped with photodegradation ability, excluding contaminated toxic metal ions from Shahu lake water for efficient clean water recovery.

Supporting information. The supporting information (SI) file contains 03 Supporting Notes, 13 Supporting Figures (Figure S1 – S14), 06 Supporting Videos, and 02 Supporting Tables (Table S1– S2). The material characterization tools information, photothermal conversion efficiency calculation and heat losses, detailed theoretical calculations & discussions of salt-rejection assumptions, schematic illustration of the fabrication of MnCDs@PPy, FESEM image of super hydrophilic MnO2 NWs and CDs modified MnO2 NWs, TEM image of super hydrophilic MnO2 NWs and CDs modified MnO2 NWs, TEM image of pure carbon dots (CDs), High resolution EDS mapping of CDs modified MnO2 NWs, elemental composition of CDs modified MnO2 NWs, Air permeability test of MnCDs@PPy, Mass change profile of MnCDs@PPy under different solar irradiance, surface temperature profile of MnCDs@PPy under different solar irradiance, non-woven cotton wrapped prototype of MnCDs@PPy, comparative illustration of diffusion and advection phenomena, nano-filtration of heavy metal ions rejection through from Shahu lake water, antibacterial activity of MnO2 NWs, CDs modified MnO2 NWs, and MnCDs@PPy, The detailed comparison of mass change with other recent successful solar-driven systems, inhibition zone measurement of MnO2 NWs, CDs modified MnO2 NWs, and MnCDs@PPy against different bacterial pathogens.
